summary: Gruber dissects a Bloomberg report by Mark Gurman on Apple's rumored Siri speaker, arguing it contains almost no actual news despite being 800 words long. He points out that the report's single piece of news — that manufacturing has started — could be summarized in 13 words, and that analyst Ming-Chi Kuo and leaker Sonny Dickson had more detailed information weeks earlier. Gruber contrasts this thin reporting with Gurman's genuinely impressive AirPods scoop from 2016, which had accurate details nine months before announcement. He closes by noting that with WWDC five days away, almost nothing about iOS 11, macOS 10.13, or new hardware has leaked, suggesting Tim Cook deserves credit for tightening Apple's secrecy.
key_points: The Bloomberg report stretches one sentence of actual news — that Apple's Siri speaker entered manufacturing — into an 800-word feature article
Gurman's report lacks basic details (appearance, size, display) and trails Ming-Chi Kuo and Sonny Dickson by weeks on specifics
The thin Siri speaker reporting contrasts sharply with Gurman's genuinely detailed AirPods scoop nine months before Apple's announcement
With WWDC five days away, virtually nothing about iOS 11, macOS 10.13, or new hardware has leaked, suggesting Apple's secrecy efforts are working
Kuo and Bloomberg's report conflict on whether the speaker will have a touch panel, setting up future 'claim chowder'
money_quotes: Seriously, that's about it for news about the product.
The keynote is five days away and Gurman and Webb don't know if it's going to be announced.
What does it look like? How big is it? Does it have a display? Crickets.
There's a 13-word summary with all the actual news in this story.
It's painful to see these meager stale morsels stretched into feature articles.
AirPods — now that was a scoop.
It might be time to give Tim Cook credit for 'doubling down on secrecy'.
key_insight: When Apple's secrecy machine is working well, even the best-connected reporters are reduced to stretching thin morsels into feature articles — and the contrast with their genuine past scoops makes the thinness all the more obvious.
